text: Intracellular peptide hormone receptors bind their ligands primarily as α-helices 1–4 and thus sensitive detection methods for peptide hormones would be valuable for clinical applications of a number of endocrine diseases 5 . De novo protein design has demonstrated the feasibility of designing binders to large, structured proteins with high affinity and specificity 6,7 . However, designing high affinity interactions between proteins and short sequences of peptides that are intrinsically helical is a challenge that remains to be addressed. Here, we describe a parametric approach and a suite of deep learning tools for designing such interactions. We apply an extension of the RFdiffusion method 8 to design a binder to flexible targets, and apply a method of partial diffusion by iteratively noising and denoising the input structures to generate picomolar affinity binders to short helical peptides. These can be generated from either optimized designs derived from other design approaches or from scratch from a random noise distribution of no experimental value that requires adjustment. Designs generated with our method enable the enrichment and detection of parathyroid hormone and glucagon by mass spectrometry, as well as the construction of bioluminescent protein biosensors. Our findings for designing binders to flexible targets and optimizing both natural and designed proteins by a process of partial diffusion should be widely applicable.
